title: Raw dog and cat food recalled over Salmonella and Listeria concerns
summary: A recall has been issued for raw dog and cat food sold in Illinois and other states due to potential Salmonella and Listeria contamination. The affected products are marketed as raw pet food for both dogs and cats. Salmonella can cause gastrointestinal illness in pets, including vomiting, diarrhea, fever, and lethargy, and can also spread to humans who handle contaminated food or come into contact with infected animals. Listeria poses additional health risks, particularly to young, elderly, pregnant, or immunocompromised pets and people. Pet owners who purchased the recalled raw food should check their supply, stop feeding it immediately, and follow official recall instructions. They should also thoroughly clean and disinfect bowls, surfaces, utensils, and hands after handling the product. The recall was reported by Food Poison Journal on August 25, 2025, and by NBC 5 Chicago on August 27, 2025. Specific brand names, lot numbers, and UPC codes were not detailed in the available reports, so consumers should consult the FDA or the manufacturer for complete recall information.
bodyHtml:
What happened

A recall has been issued for raw dog and cat food due to potential Salmonella and Listeria contamination. The issue was first reported by Food Poison Journal on August 25, 2025, and later covered by NBC 5 Chicago on August 27, 2025. The recalled products were sold in Illinois and other states.

Affected products

The reports indicate that raw dog and cat food is affected. The Food Poison Journal report cites Salmonella, while the NBC 5 Chicago report mentions both Salmonella and Listeria. Specific brand names, product names, lot numbers, UPC codes, and package sizes were not provided in the available source material.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs and cats that consumed the recalled raw food may be at risk. Pets infected with Salmonella may show signs such as vomiting, diarrhea, fever, lethargy, decreased appetite, and abdominal pain. Listeria infection can cause similar symptoms and may be more serious in young, elderly, pregnant, or immunocompromised animals.

Why it matters

Salmonella and Listeria are zoonotic pathogens, meaning they can spread from animals to humans. Pet owners who handle contaminated raw food or clean up after infected pets can become ill. Children, older adults, pregnant individuals, and people with weakened immune systems are especially vulnerable. Raw pet food has a higher risk of bacterial contamination compared to cooked or processed products.

What pet owners should do

Stop feeding the recalled product immediately. Secure it in a sealed container so children and pets cannot access it. Thoroughly wash bowls, utensils, surfaces, and hands with hot soapy water after handling raw pet food. Monitor pets for symptoms of illness and contact a veterinarian if they show signs. For refund and recall instructions, check the FDA recall website or contact the manufacturer or retailer where the product was purchased. The NBC 5 Chicago and Food Poison Journal reports did not include specific recall contact details.

title: FDA Warns Xylitol Is Dangerous for Dogs
summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has issued a safety warning advising dog owners to keep xylitol away from their pets. Xylitol is a sugar substitute commonly used in sugar-free gum, candy, baked goods, peanut butter, toothpaste, and some medications. The FDA emphasizes that xylitol can be extremely dangerous and potentially life-threatening for dogs. The warning covers dog owners generally and does not identify a specific brand, product, or geographic limitation. Unlike sugar, xylitol is rapidly absorbed in dogs and can trigger a sudden release of insulin, leading to hypoglycemia, seizures, liver failure, or death. Because xylitol is found in many everyday household items, the FDA warns pet owners to check product labels, keep xylitol-containing products out of reach, and seek immediate veterinary care if a dog ingests any amount. Symptoms may include vomiting, weakness, loss of coordination, and seizures. Pet owners should not attempt to induce vomiting without veterinary guidance and should contact a veterinarian or animal poison control center immediately.
bodyHtml:
What happened

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) published a warning titled "Paws Off Xylitol; It's Dangerous for Dogs." The alert informs pet owners that xylitol, a common sugar substitute, poses a serious toxicity risk to dogs and should be kept away from them.

Affected products

The available FDA report does not name a specific brand, product, lot number, or UPC. Xylitol is frequently found in sugar-free gum, mints, candy, baked goods, peanut butter, toothpaste, mouthwash, chewable vitamins, and some medications or liquid suspensions. Pet owners should check ingredient labels on any product marketed as sugar-free or reduced-sugar.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs are the animals identified in the FDA warning as being at risk from xylitol exposure.

Why it matters

In dogs, xylitol is absorbed quickly and can cause a sudden release of insulin. This rapid insulin spike can lead to hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), which may progress to seizures, liver failure, or death. The FDA warning highlights that even small amounts of xylitol can be dangerous, making prevention and rapid response critical.

What pet owners should do

  • Read product labels and avoid giving dogs foods or items containing xylitol.
  • Store xylitol-containing products in secure locations out of a dog's reach.
  • If a dog ingests xylitol, contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately.
  • Do not induce vomiting unless instructed by a veterinary professional.
  • Watch for symptoms such as vomiting, weakness, stumbling, loss of coordination, tremors, or seizures, and seek emergency care if they appear.

title: Pet Poison Helpline: Acetaminophen enters top 10 pet poisons of 2025
summary: Pet Poison Helpline released its annual “Top 10 Pet Poisons of 2025” list, reporting that acetaminophen—the active ingredient in many over-the-counter pain and fever medications—has moved into the top 10 most common pet toxins for the year. The ranking is based on cases handled by the helpline’s toxicology experts. While the full list covers thousands of exposures tracked by the service, the rise of acetaminophen is notable because even small amounts can cause serious, sometimes fatal, poisoning in dogs and cats. Cats are especially vulnerable due to their limited ability to metabolize the drug. The alert is relevant to dog and cat owners across the United States and Canada who keep human medications in the home. Pet Poison Helpline urges owners to store all medications in pet-proof containers, avoid giving pets any human drug without veterinary guidance, and seek immediate help if an animal may have ingested a pill or capsule. Contact your veterinarian or a pet poison control center right away, and have the medication packaging available.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On January 15, 2026, Pet Poison Helpline published its annual “Top 10 Pet Poisons of 2025” report, summarizing the most common toxin exposures the service handled during the year. News outlets including DVM360 and PR Newswire covered the release. The headline finding was that acetaminophen has risen into the top 10 most common pet poisons for 2025, reflecting a notable increase in reported cases.

Affected products

  • Pet Poison Helpline “Top 10 Pet Poisons of 2025” report (annual toxicity ranking)
  • Acetaminophen-containing products — over-the-counter pain and fever medications that contain acetaminophen (the reports did not list specific brands, lot numbers, or UPCs)

Which pets are at risk

The alert is primarily relevant to dogs and cats. Cats are especially sensitive because they lack the liver enzymes needed to safely break down acetaminophen, making even a single tablet potentially life-threatening. Dogs are also at risk at higher doses, and other small companion animals may be affected if exposed.

Why it matters

Acetaminophen is not safe for pets. Ingestion can cause liver damage, methemoglobinemia, and destruction of red blood cells, leading to oxygen deprivation, organ failure, and death if treatment is delayed. The fact that it climbed into the top 10 suggests that more pet owners are leaving medications within reach or accidentally sharing human drugs with animals.

What pet owners should do

  • Store all human medications in locked, pet-proof cabinets or containers.
  • Never give pets any over-the-counter or prescription human medication without veterinary approval.
  • If you suspect your pet has swallowed acetaminophen or any unknown medication, contact your veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.
  • Have the medication packaging, strength, and estimated amount ingested ready when you call.

title: Vet warnings: Daffodils, rhododendrons and lesser-known toxin risk to dogs and cats
summary: Veterinary and media outlets have issued a series of early-spring toxicity warnings for pet owners. Reports from Wales Online, Vet Times, and the Derry Journal highlight that common garden plants and a lesser-known toxin can seriously harm companion animals. Daffodils (Narcissus species) are described as “dangerously toxic” to dogs and cats; all parts of the plant—especially the bulbs—contain lycorine and calcium oxalate crystals that can cause vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, abdominal pain, and, in severe cases, cardiac arrhythmias or tremors. Rhododendron and azalea plants contain grayanotoxins that disrupt sodium channels and can produce vomiting, weakness, collapse, and abnormal heart rhythms in pets. A separate Wales Online report cautions dog owners not to delay veterinary care if they suspect exposure to a “lesser known toxin.” These warnings are relevant across the UK and Ireland, with general veterinary guidance from Vet Times. No commercial product or lot number is involved; the advisories center on household and garden plants. Pet owners should keep dogs and cats away from these plants, remove chewed leaves or bulbs, and seek immediate veterinary attention if any symptoms appear.
bodyHtml:
What happened

In February 2026, veterinary and news sources published separate but related warnings about toxins that endanger pets. A Derry Journal article described daffodils as “dangerously toxic” to dogs and cats, while Vet Times highlighted rhododendron and azalea toxicity. Wales Online reported a vet warning urging dog owners not to delay treatment after a suspected “lesser known toxin” exposure. Together these reports point to heightened springtime risk from garden plants and environmental toxins.

Affected products

No pet food or commercial product is recalled. The hazards are biological toxins found in:

  • Daffodils (Narcissus species) — especially the bulbs; contain lycorine and calcium oxalate crystals.
  • Rhododendron (Rhododendron L) and azalea (Azalea species) — contain grayanotoxins.
  • An unspecified “lesser known toxin” flagged in the Wales Online report.

Lot/UPC information is not applicable.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs and cats are both at risk from daffodils and rhododendron/azalea plants. The Wales Online warning focuses on dogs, but the underlying principle applies to other pets.

Why it matters

Ingesting even small amounts can cause severe gastrointestinal, neurologic, and cardiac effects. Delayed treatment may worsen outcomes, especially for grayanotoxin and lycorine poisoning.

What pet owners should do

Prevent access: keep pets away from flower beds, vases, and compost containing bulbs. If a pet chews a plant or shows drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, tremors, or collapse, contact a veterinarian immediately. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen. Bring a sample or photo of the plant to help identify the toxin. There is no product recall contact; for general guidance, consult your veterinary clinic or a pet poison helpline.

title: Xylitol in Gum and Medications Poses Poisoning Risk to Pets
summary: News reports from MSN and CBS News on February 26, 2026, warn that xylitol—a sugar substitute common in sugar-free gum and some medications—poses a serious poisoning risk to pets. No specific brand, product line, or recall was announced in the coverage; the stories are a general safety alert. The reports note that animals such as cats and other pets can be affected. In pets, xylitol can trigger a rapid release of insulin, leading to hypoglycemia, vomiting, loss of coordination, seizures, and potentially fatal liver failure. The geographic scope was not specified, but the reports were published for a general audience. Pet owners should treat xylitol like a household toxin: keep gum, medications, toothpaste, baked goods, and other sugar-free products containing xylitol out of reach, read ingredient labels carefully, and seek immediate veterinary care or contact a poison control center if they suspect a pet has ingested xylitol.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On February 26, 2026, MSN and CBS News published reports warning that xylitol—a sugar substitute frequently found in sugar-free gum and certain medications—can be dangerous to pets. The coverage appears to be a general pet-safety alert rather than a notice of a specific product recall or brand-linked incident.

Affected products

The reports identify xylitol as the substance of concern and note that it is commonly used in sugar-free gum and medications. No specific brands, product names, lot numbers, or UPCs were provided in the available coverage.

Which pets are at risk

CBS News lists cats as affected animals, while MSN categorizes the affected animals as "other." Dogs are widely recognized as being especially vulnerable to xylitol poisoning; any pet that ingests xylitol should be considered at risk and evaluated promptly.

Why it matters

Xylitol is highly toxic to many animals. Ingestion can cause a sudden release of insulin, resulting in dangerously low blood sugar (hypoglycemia), vomiting, weakness, loss of coordination, tremors, seizures, and potentially liver failure or death. Because symptoms can develop quickly, early recognition and treatment are critical.

What pet owners should do

Keep all xylitol-containing products—such as sugar-free gum, mints, candy, baked goods, peanut butter, toothpaste, and medications—well out of reach of pets. Read labels carefully, and avoid giving pets any human food or medication unless approved by a veterinarian. If a pet is suspected of ingesting xylitol, contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control hotline immediately; do not induce vomiting unless instructed by a professional. Watch for signs of weakness, vomiting, tremors, or seizures and seek emergency care without delay.

title: RCL Foods pet food and dog treats recalled over Salmonella risk
summary: RCL Foods is recalling pet food and dog treats because of possible Salmonella contamination. The recalled items are described as dog treats and pet food; specific product names, lot numbers, and UPC codes were not disclosed in the reports. The contamination poses a risk to dogs and other pets that eat the food, potentially causing vomiting, diarrhea, fever, and lethargy, and can also sicken humans who handle the product. The geographic scope of the recall was not specified. Pet owners should immediately stop feeding the affected food, thoroughly wash hands, bowls, and surfaces that touched it, and contact RCL Foods or the retailer for disposal and refund instructions. Any pet showing signs of illness should be seen by a veterinarian.
bodyHtml:
What happened

RCL Foods has recalled pet food and dog treats due to potential Salmonella contamination. Reports covering the recall describe it as a Salmonella-related pet food recall; specific timing of distribution or first report date was not detailed beyond the news coverage dates.

Affected products

The recalled products are described as RCL Foods pet food and dog treats. The reports did not include specific product names, package sizes, lot numbers, UPC codes, or best-by dates. Pet owners should check any RCL Foods pet food or dog treats in their home and compare them with the company’s official recall notice or retailer announcements.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs and potentially other pets that consume the contaminated food are at risk. Salmonella infection can cause vomiting, diarrhea (sometimes bloody), fever, lethargy, loss of appetite, and dehydration. Some animals may carry the bacteria without showing symptoms and can still spread it to people and other animals.

Why it matters

Salmonella can sicken both pets and people. Even healthy-looking pets can shed the bacteria, putting vulnerable humans—especially young children, elderly individuals, and immunocompromised people—at risk through contact with contaminated food, bowls, or animal waste.

What pet owners should do

Stop feeding the recalled product immediately. Wash your hands, pet bowls, utensils, and any surfaces that touched the food. Monitor pets for illness and contact a veterinarian if symptoms appear. Reach out to RCL Foods or the retailer where the product was purchased for refund or disposal instructions. The reports did not provide a specific recall hotline or website.

title: Toxic PFAS detected in wide range of dog and cat food in Japan
summary: A research report covered by Mainichi on March 2, 2026, found toxic PFAS in a wide range of dog and cat food sold in Japan. The report did not name specific brands, product lines, lot numbers, or concentration levels, so the full scope of the contamination is still unclear. Affected animals are dogs and cats that consume the implicated foods. PFAS, or per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, are persistent "forever chemicals" that can accumulate in the body and have been linked to kidney, liver, thyroid, immune, and developmental problems. Because no product recall or official safety advisory has been announced in the available information, pet owners should monitor updates from Japanese regulators and manufacturers, review the food they are feeding, and consult a veterinarian if their pets show signs of illness such as vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, or appetite loss. Those who are concerned may also consider switching to foods that have documented third-party safety or contaminant testing.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On March 2, 2026, Mainichi reported that research had detected toxic PFAS in a wide range of dog and cat food sold in Japan. PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) are a large family of synthetic, highly persistent chemicals sometimes called "forever chemicals" because they do not break down easily in the environment or in living tissues. The available report indicates the contamination was found across multiple pet food products, but it does not provide details on the source of the contamination, the measured concentrations, or how the samples were selected.

Affected products

The report refers to a "wide range of dog and cat food" sold in Japan. Specific brand names, product names, formulas, UPC codes, lot numbers, and expiration dates were not disclosed in the available coverage. Pet owners should watch for follow-up announcements from Japanese regulators, manufacturers, or the research team that may identify particular products or batches.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs and cats that eat the contaminated foods are the animals at risk. Because pets often eat the same commercial food every day, ongoing exposure to low levels of PFAS could lead to accumulation over time. Smaller animals and those with pre-existing kidney, liver, or immune conditions may be especially vulnerable.

Why it matters

PFAS are known to be persistent, bioaccumulative, and potentially toxic. In animals and humans, they have been associated with kidney and liver damage, thyroid disruption, immune-system effects, developmental problems, and certain cancers. Pet food can be an important exposure pathway for household animals, and pets may also serve as sentinels for human exposure because they share living environments and may consume similar food ingredients.

What pet owners should do

  • Monitor official channels: Keep an eye on announcements from Japan's Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare, the Ministry of the Environment, and pet food manufacturers for brand-specific recalls or safety guidance.
  • Watch your pet: Seek veterinary care if your dog or cat shows vomiting, diarrhea, loss of appetite, lethargy, increased thirst, or other unexplained symptoms.
  • Review your pet's food: Check the label and packaging of the products you are feeding. If a specific brand or lot is later identified, stop using it and follow the manufacturer's or regulator's instructions.
  • Consider alternatives: If you are concerned, talk to your veterinarian about switching to a food that has been tested for PFAS or other contaminants, or that comes from suppliers with transparent sourcing and quality control.
  • Do not panic-change without guidance: Abrupt diet changes can cause digestive upset, so transition foods gradually under veterinary advice.

title: Pet Poison Helpline warns Easter baskets and lilies threaten pets
summary: Pet Poison Helpline, a veterinary toxicology service, issued a holiday safety alert on March 20, 2026, warning that Easter baskets and seasonal decorations can expose pets to life-threatening hazards. The affected items include chocolate and xylitol-sweetened candies, true lilies used in floral arrangements, plastic Easter grass, and small toys or basket fillers. Cats are especially at risk from lilies, which can cause fatal kidney failure from small ingestions of petals, leaves, pollen, or vase water. Dogs are particularly vulnerable to chocolate and xylitol, which can trigger vomiting, hypoglycemia, seizures, and worse. Any pet that chews plastic grass or swallows small toys can suffer an intestinal blockage. The alert applies broadly to households celebrating Easter, with no specific geographic limits. Pet owners should keep baskets, candy, and floral arrangements out of reach, avoid lilies entirely in homes with cats, choose pet-safe flowers, and supervise pets during gatherings. If a pet ingests any suspect item, owners should contact a veterinarian or the Pet Poison Helpline immediately and have the product or packaging available.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On March 20, 2026, Pet Poison Helpline published an Easter safety alert titled "Is Your Easter Basket Pet-Safe?" The alert warned that holiday baskets, candy, and floral arrangements can introduce dangerous toxins and choking or obstruction hazards into homes with pets.

Affected products

  • Pet Poison Helpline alert: Easter baskets and seasonal decorations.
  • Chocolate and xylitol-containing candies (common in Easter treats).
  • True lilies (Lilium and Hemerocallis species) in floral arrangements.
  • Plastic Easter grass and small basket toys or fillers.

No brand names, lot numbers, or UPCs were provided.

Which pets are at risk

  • Cats: Highly susceptible to lily-induced kidney failure; all parts of the plant, including pollen and vase water, are toxic.
  • Dogs: At risk from chocolate (theobromine and caffeine) and xylitol, which can cause vomiting, seizures, hypoglycemia, liver failure, or death.
  • Dogs, cats, and other pets: Plastic grass and small toys can cause intestinal blockages or choking.

Why it matters

Easter celebrations bring new items into the home, and pets often encounter them through curiosity, unsecured baskets, or dropped candy. Lilies are one of the most dangerous plants for cats, and even tiny amounts can be fatal without prompt treatment. Xylitol and chocolate are among the most common canine poisonings during holidays. Early recognition and rapid veterinary intervention can be life-saving.

What pet owners should do

  • Keep Easter baskets, candy, and floral arrangements in a secure, pet-free location.
  • Do not bring lilies into homes with cats; choose non-toxic alternatives such as roses, sunflowers, or orchids, and confirm pet safety before purchase.
  • Supervise pets during gatherings and pick up dropped food or decorations immediately.
  • Contact a veterinarian or the Pet Poison Helpline at 1-855-764-7661 if ingestion is suspected; have the product or packaging ready.
  • For severe symptoms such as collapse, seizures, or repeated vomiting, seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

title: Toxic spring flowers, including tulips, pose risk to cats and dogs
summary: Multiple pet-safety outlets are warning that common spring flowers and plants can poison cats and dogs. Reports from Yahoo Lifestyle Canada, The Spruce Pets, Daily Paws, and the ASPCA flag seasonal blooms—especially tulips—as a hazard when pets chew, ingest, or contact them. The warnings cover both indoor bouquets and garden plantings, with Yahoo Lifestyle Canada noting the issue in Canada. While the articles do not describe a single product recall, they identify a recurring spring-time risk: certain flowers contain toxins that can cause drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, oral irritation, and more serious effects depending on the plant and amount eaten. Cats appear to be particularly vulnerable, but dogs are also at risk. Pet owners should identify which flowers are toxic before buying bouquets or planting gardens, keep plants out of reach, and seek immediate veterinary care or contact a poison-control hotline if a pet chews or swallows any suspect bloom. The ASPCA, veterinarians, and pet publications all advise choosing pet-safe alternatives and reviewing household plants regularly as spring arrives.
bodyHtml:
What happened

In April 2026, several pet-health and lifestyle outlets issued warnings about the dangers common spring flowers pose to pets. Yahoo Lifestyle Canada reported that “these spring flowers may be toxic to cats and dogs,” The Spruce Pets asked “Are Tulips Toxic to Cats?,” Daily Paws highlighted “5 Flowers Are Toxic to Cats,” and the ASPCA published expert guidance on flowers and plants toxic to pets. The coverage does not describe a single product recall; instead, it flags a seasonal hazard as households bring fresh blooms indoors and plant gardens.

Affected products

No specific brand, lot, or UPC is involved. The reports identify categories of spring flowers and plants, with tulips specifically named by The Spruce Pets and other seasonal blooms flagged by Daily Paws and Yahoo Lifestyle Canada. The ASPCA guidance covers a broad range of flowers and plants hazardous to pets.

Which pets are at risk

Cats and dogs are both at risk, though cats are emphasized in several of the warnings. The Yahoo Lifestyle Canada report specifically mentions both cats and dogs in Canada; The Spruce Pets and Daily Paws focus on cats; the ASPCA addresses pets generally.

Why it matters

Ingestion or contact with toxic plants can cause drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, mouth irritation, and more serious health problems depending on the plant species and amount consumed. Because spring bouquets and garden plantings are common during this season, pets may have easier access to dangerous foliage.

What pet owners should do

  • Verify every plant and flower before bringing it home or planting it.
  • Keep bouquets, planters, and garden beds out of reach of pets.
  • If a pet chews or swallows a suspect flower, contact a veterinarian or poison-control hotline immediately. The ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center can be reached at (888) 426-4435 (fees may apply).
  • Choose pet-safe alternatives such as spider plants, Boston ferns, roses, or cat grass.
  • Regularly inspect indoor and outdoor spaces for fallen petals, leaves, or bulbs.

title: Pet toxicity warnings: lilies, alstroemeria, essential oils, and common plants
summary: Multiple pet-safety reports published in May 2026 warned that common household and garden items pose serious toxicity risks to dogs and cats. Catster addressed whether alstroemeria is toxic to cats, while Vet Times emphasized that lilies are lethally toxic to cats but not to dogs. Forbes examined whether any essential oils are safe for dogs and cats, and Times Union listed 17 plants toxic to both species with identification guidance. The reports collectively highlight that cats are especially vulnerable to certain plant toxins, with lilies singled out as potentially fatal. Essential oils are also flagged as a concern for both dogs and cats. No specific product brand, lot number, or recall contact details were disclosed in the available coverage. Pet owners are advised to identify and remove dangerous plants and oils from pet-accessible areas, monitor for symptoms of poisoning such as vomiting, drooling, or lethargy, and contact a veterinarian or pet poison helpline immediately if exposure is suspected.
bodyHtml:
What happened

In May 2026, several pet-safety publications issued warnings about everyday items that can poison companion animals. Catster published vet-approved advice on alstroemeria and cats, Vet Times stressed that lilies are lethal to cats (though not toxic to dogs), Forbes explored whether pet-safe essential oils exist for dogs and cats, and Times Union profiled 17 plants dangerous to both species with tips on how to identify them. The coverage did not describe a single product recall or branded incident, but rather a cluster of educational alerts about common toxins.

Affected products (include brand, product names, lot/UPC if available)

No specific brand, product lot, or UPC information was provided in the reports. The substances and items discussed include:

  • Alstroemeria (Peruvian lily)
  • Lilies (noted as lethally toxic to cats)
  • Essential oils
  • 17 additional plants identified as toxic to dogs and cats by Times Union

Which pets are at risk

  • Cats are at risk from alstroemeria and lilies; lilies are described as potentially fatal to cats.
  • Dogs and cats are both at risk from certain essential oils and from the 17 plants listed by Times Union.
  • Dogs are not affected by lily toxicity in the same way as cats, according to Vet Times.

Why it matters

These items are common in homes, gardens, and floral arrangements, making accidental exposure likely. Lilies in particular are a well-documented, life-threatening hazard to cats even in small amounts. Because many pet owners may not realize that plants marketed as ornamental or products sold as "natural" (such as essential oils) can be dangerous, the alerts aim to prevent avoidable poisonings.

What pet owners should do (include recall contact / refund info if known)

No recall, refund, or manufacturer contact information was available in the reports. Owners should:

  • Identify all plants and essential oils in the home and yard.
  • Keep lilies, alstroemeria, and other toxic plants out of reach of pets, or remove them entirely—especially in households with cats.
  • Use essential oils only with veterinary guidance, and ensure pets are not exposed to diffused or concentrated products.
  • Watch for poisoning symptoms such as vomiting, drooling, difficulty breathing, lethargy, or changes in appetite or urination.
  • If a pet chews, ingests, or is exposed to a suspected toxin, contact a veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.

title: Albright’s Raw Pet Food chicken recipe recalled over Salmonella risk
summary: Albright’s Raw Pet Food, an Indiana-based raw pet food company, announced a voluntary recall of its select chicken recipe for dogs in early May 2026. The recall was prompted by potential Salmonella contamination and was reported by outlets including KSNT, MLive, USA Today, and Fox Business, with the FDA listed as the source of the alert. Salmonella can sicken dogs and humans; dogs may develop lethargy, diarrhea, vomiting, fever, and decreased appetite, while people can be exposed through handling raw food or contaminated surfaces. The recalled product was sold both online and in stores, with distribution described as nationwide or in at least six states. Affected animals are dogs. Pet owners should stop feeding the food, discard it safely, wash hands and bowls thoroughly, and contact the company for a refund. No specific lot numbers or UPCs were provided in the available reports; owners should consult the FDA recall notice or the company’s website for identifying details and seek veterinary care if symptoms appear.
bodyHtml:
What happened

Albright’s Raw Pet Food issued a voluntary recall of a select chicken recipe for dogs in early May 2026 after potential Salmonella contamination was identified. Multiple news outlets, including KSNT 27 News, MLive.com, FOX 10 Phoenix, KTLA, and USA Today, reported the alert beginning May 7, 2026, with some citing the FDA. The company is identified in several reports as an Indiana-based raw pet food manufacturer. No specific human or pet illness counts were included in the available summaries, but the recall was described as a precaution against Salmonella risk.

Affected products

  • Brand: Albright’s Raw Pet Food
  • Product: Select chicken recipe for dogs (raw dog food)
  • Lot numbers, UPCs, package sizes, and expiration dates: Not included in the provided summaries. Pet owners should check the FDA recall notice or the company’s official announcement for identifying product details.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs are the affected animals. Salmonella can cause salmonellosis in dogs, with symptoms that may include lethargy, diarrhea (sometimes bloody), vomiting, fever, decreased appetite, and abdominal discomfort. Young puppies, elderly dogs, and dogs with weakened immune systems are at higher risk of severe illness. Humans who handle raw pet food, contaminated bowls, or surfaces can also become ill, particularly children, older adults, pregnant individuals, and immunocompromised people.

Why it matters

Salmonella is a zoonotic bacterium that can spread between animals and people. Raw pet food is more likely to harbor pathogens because it is not cooked to kill bacteria. Reports described the recalled product as being sold online and in stores, with distribution characterized as either nationwide or in at least six states. Because raw food can contaminate kitchen surfaces, feeding bowls, and hands, the risk extends beyond the dog eating the food.

What pet owners should do

  • Stop feeding the recalled Albright’s chicken recipe for dogs immediately.
  • Discard any remaining product in a sealed bag so it cannot be eaten by pets or wildlife.
  • Wash all bowls, utensils, feeding mats, and surfaces that contacted the food with hot, soapy water.
  • Wash hands thoroughly after handling the food or cleaning supplies.
  • Monitor dogs for signs of Salmonella infection and contact a veterinarian if symptoms develop.
  • Contact Albright’s Raw Pet Food or the retailer where the product was purchased for refund or replacement instructions.
  • For the latest lot numbers, UPCs, and geographic distribution, consult the FDA recall notice or the company’s website.

title: Dog food recalled across East Coast after listeria threat and pet illnesses
summary: A multi-state dog food recall has been announced for products distributed across the East Coast after reports of illness in pets. The recalled dog food, which is sold frozen or refrigerated and may be stored in household freezers, is being pulled from shelves in at least six states because of potential Listeria monocytogenes contamination. The brand and specific product names were not disclosed in the available reports, but the recall is described as urgent and affects dogs. Listeria can cause serious illness in dogs—symptoms include vomiting, diarrhea, fever, lethargy, and loss of appetite—and poses a risk to humans who handle contaminated food or surfaces. Pet owners who have the recalled product should stop feeding it immediately, seal and discard it, wash hands and any bowls or storage areas, and watch their dogs for signs of illness. Anyone whose pet becomes ill after eating the food should contact a veterinarian promptly. Retailers and the manufacturer should provide refund or replacement instructions through their recall notice once full details are released.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On May 8, 2026, MassLive reported an urgent dog food recall affecting six states. By May 23, the incident was described as a major East Coast recall involving a Listeria threat to both dogs and humans. A May 26 follow-up noted the recall came after several pet illnesses. The reports indicate a dog food product—likely frozen or refrigerated, given the advice to “check freezers”—was distributed across the East Coast and is being pulled from the market because of potential Listeria monocytogenes contamination.

Affected products

The available reports do not identify the brand, specific product names, lot codes, or UPCs. The recalled item is a dog food product sold in at least six states on the East Coast. Because the public advisories tell consumers to check freezers, the product is likely sold frozen or refrigerated.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs are the primary animals at risk. Listeria can cause gastrointestinal and systemic illness in dogs, especially puppies, seniors, or those with weakened immune systems.

Why it matters

Listeria monocytogenes is a bacterium that can sicken pets and humans. Dogs may show vomiting, diarrhea, fever, lethargy, and loss of appetite. Humans can be infected through handling contaminated food or touching surfaces. In people, listeriosis can be severe for pregnant women, the elderly, young children, and immunocompromised individuals.

What pet owners should do

Stop feeding the recalled food immediately. Seal the product and discard it in a covered trash container so pets and wildlife cannot access it. Wash hands, bowls, scoops, and any storage areas that touched the food with hot soapy water. Monitor dogs for illness and call a veterinarian if symptoms appear. Since brand and contact details were not provided in the reports, check the FDA and manufacturer recall notices for refund instructions and a full product list.

title: Common plants pose toxicity risk to cats, dogs, and birds
summary: Multiple pet-safety reports published in June 2026 highlight that common garden and houseplants can be toxic to pets, particularly cats. Lilies are specifically identified as dangerous to cats, and Farmers' Almanac published an A-to-Z guide covering 30 common houseplants hazardous to cats, dogs, and birds. The reports focus on the United States and are intended to help pet owners recognize and avoid plants that can cause serious illness or death if ingested, licked, or contacted. No specific product recall is associated with these reports; rather, they are consumer safety advisories. Pet owners should review indoor and outdoor plants, remove or relocate toxic species out of reach, and contact a veterinarian or pet poison helpline immediately if a pet chews leaves, flowers, pollen, or stems. Keeping a list of toxic plant names available can help with rapid identification during emergencies.

What happened

In June 2026, several pet-safety publications issued warnings about plant toxicity in companion animals. USA Today published "Planting a garden? Skip these plants toxic to cats," advising cat owners to avoid certain garden plants. Encyclopaedia Britannica released "Why Are Lilies Toxic to Cats?" examining the danger lilies pose to felines. Farmers' Almanac published "30 Common Houseplants Toxic to Pets (A to Z): Cat, Dog, and Bird Safety Guide," listing hazardous indoor plants. These reports all address the same core concern: common ornamental plants can poison pets.

Affected products

No commercial product recall is involved. The reports identify broad categories of plants:

  • Garden plants toxic to cats (USA Today)
  • Lilies, specifically noted as dangerous to cats (Encyclopaedia Britannica)
  • 30 common houseplants listed in Farmers' Almanac's A-to-Z guide, covering risks to cats, dogs, and birds Specific plant names, lot numbers, or UPCs are not provided in the available summaries.

Which pets are at risk

Cats are the primary focus of the USA Today and Britannica reports. Dogs and birds are also at risk according to the Farmers' Almanac guide, which covers cats, dogs, and birds.

Why it matters

Plant toxicity is a preventable cause of pet illness and death. Cats are especially vulnerable to certain plants, and lilies in particular can cause severe kidney damage. Because pets may chew on leaves, flowers, or stems, awareness of which plants are hazardous is essential for prevention.

What pet owners should do

  • Audit indoor and outdoor plants against the reported guides.
  • Remove toxic plants or place them completely out of reach.
  • Prevent pets from accessing water from vases containing cut flowers.
  • If ingestion or contact is suspected, contact a veterinarian immediately or call a pet poison helpline.
  • Keep plant names and photos available for rapid identification during emergencies.

No recall contact or refund information is available because this is not a product recall.

bodyHtml:
What happened

On June 19, 2026, KTLA reported that more than 400,000 pounds of pet food were being recalled across several states. The initial report appears to be a brief alert, and it does not provide specifics about the manufacturer, brand, or triggering incident. Pet owners should treat the alert as a developing story until official recall details are published.

Affected products

The available reports do not identify the brand, product names, lot numbers, UPC codes, package sizes, or expiration dates involved in the recall. The only quantity disclosed is "over 400,000 lbs." Because the product identifiers are not yet available, consumers cannot yet verify whether a specific bag, can, or pouch of pet food is affected.

Which pets are at risk

The reports classify affected animals as "other," a label typically used when a recall applies to companion animals but the exact species has not been specified. The product could potentially affect dogs, cats, or other household pets. Without further information from the manufacturer or FDA, it is not possible to confirm which animals are most at risk.

Why it matters

A recall of more than 400,000 pounds distributed across multiple states represents a large-scale pet food incident. Even though the specific hazard has not been disclosed, any large recall raises the possibility of contamination, mislabeling, or nutritional problems that could harm pets. Prompt, accurate information is essential so owners can remove unsafe products from their homes.

What pet owners should do

  • Monitor official recall channels such as the FDA's pet food recall list and the websites of major pet food manufacturers for the brand and lot details.
  • Do not feed any pet food that appears on a subsequent recall announcement matching the description.
  • Check packaging, expiration dates, and lot numbers against future recall notices.
  • Contact a veterinarian if a pet shows symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, loss of appetite, or unusual behavior.
  • No specific recall hotline, refund process, or contact number was included in the initial reports; consumers should look for manufacturer contact information once the brand is identified.

title: Dog food sold in NY and NJ recalled over Listeria concerns
summary: A Listeria-related recall has been announced for a large quantity of pet food distributed in New York and New Jersey. An initial report on June 19, 2026 described a recall of pet food sold in NY and NJ due to Listeria concerns, while a later report on June 22, 2026 specified that more than 400,000 pounds of dog food sold in New York was affected. The brand or manufacturer behind the recalled product has not been named in the available reports, and no specific product lines, lot numbers, or UPC codes have been released. The affected animals are dogs. Pet owners who have purchased dog food in the affected region should check their supplies, avoid feeding any product tied to the recall, and monitor their pets for signs of illness. Because Listeria can cause serious illness in both animals and humans, consumers should follow guidance from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and the issuing company, retain packaging for lot-code information, and contact a veterinarian if a pet shows symptoms.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On June 19, 2026, PIX11 reported that pet food sold in New York and New Jersey was being recalled because of Listeria concerns. By June 22, 2026, hudsonvalleycountry.com reported that the recall involved more than 400,000 pounds of dog food sold in New York. The brand or manufacturer has not been identified in the available reports, and no lot numbers or UPCs have been published.

Affected products

  • Brand/Company: Not reported in available sources.
  • Product: Dog food; amount exceeds 400,000 pounds.
  • Distribution area: Sold in New York and New Jersey.
  • Specific product names, lot codes, UPCs, and expiration dates have not been released. Pet owners should watch for FDA or company recall notices and compare packaging against any updated lists.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs are the affected animals in this recall. Listeria monocytogenes can cause illness in dogs and other pets, particularly those with weakened immune systems, and may also pose a risk to humans who handle contaminated food.

Why it matters

Listeria contamination is a serious food-safety issue. In pets, Listeria can lead to vomiting, diarrhea, fever, lethargy, and neurological signs. In humans, especially those with compromised immunity, Listeria can cause severe illness. The large volume of product involved increases the chance of exposure.

What pet owners should do

  • Check dog food purchased in NY and NJ and stop feeding any product matching the recall description.
  • Retain packaging and lot/UPC information until official details are released.
  • Monitor dogs for symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, fever, or unusual behavior; contact a veterinarian if symptoms appear.
  • Wash hands and surfaces after handling pet food.
  • Follow FDA and company announcements for refund instructions and recall contact information.

title: Poisoned cat food found at Dryden mobile home park; dog treated
summary: Poisoned cat food has been discovered in a Dryden mobile home park, prompting a warning to pet owners in the Dryden, New York area. The Ithaca Voice reported that one dog was treated after the incident, while 607 News Now urged residents to keep their pets indoors. The brand or manufacturer of the cat food has not been identified, and authorities have not released details on the specific product or lot involved. The affected animals include cats and at least one dog. It appears the food had been intentionally poisoned or contaminated, but the exact substance and motive remain under investigation. The geographic scope is limited to the Dryden mobile home park where the tainted food was found, though the warning extends to surrounding neighborhoods. Pet owners should keep their pets indoors, supervise outdoor activity, and remove any unfamiliar food or items from their yards. Any animal showing signs of poisoning—such as vomiting, lethargy, drooling, tremors, or collapse—should be seen by a veterinarian immediately. There is no recall or manufacturer contact information because the product has not been identified.
bodyHtml:
What happened

In late June 2026, poisoned cat food was found in a Dryden mobile home park. The Ithaca Voice reported that one dog had been treated after the incident, suggesting either a dog ate the tainted food or was otherwise exposed. 607 News Now followed by urging residents to keep their pets indoors. Authorities have not identified the source of the food or the toxin used, and the investigation is ongoing.

Affected products

The brand, product name, lot number, and UPC of the cat food are not known. Reports describe it only as poisoned cat food left in a Dryden mobile home park. No recall or manufacturer details have been released.

Which pets are at risk

Cats and dogs are at risk, especially those with outdoor access or allowed to roam unsupervised. The affected animals include at least one dog and potentially local cats.

Why it matters

Intentional poisoning poses a serious threat to companion animals and can cause rapid illness or death. The unknown product and toxin make it difficult to predict risk or provide targeted treatment, so local pet owners should take extra precautions.

What pet owners should do

Keep pets indoors and supervise any outdoor time in the Dryden area. Remove unfamiliar food, bowls, or treats from yards. Watch for vomiting, drooling, tremors, seizures, lethargy, or collapse. If any symptoms appear, contact a veterinarian or emergency animal hospital immediately. No recall contact or refund information is available because the brand and product have not been identified.

title: Revival Animal Health expands recall of canine milk replacers over vitamin D levels
summary: Revival Animal Health, based in Orange City, Iowa, has expanded its recall of canine milk replacers after testing confirmed variable vitamin D levels. The company first issued the recall on April 17 and expanded it on June 25. The affected products include Breeder's Edge Foster Care Canine Milk Replacer, Shelter's Choice Canine Milk Replacer, and Breeder's Edge Foster Care GM (goat milk) Milk Replacer. Some lots contain too much vitamin D, while others contain too little, and elevated vitamin D was specifically confirmed in the goat milk formula. The issue affects puppies and dogs nationwide, as the products were sold online and in retail pet stores across the country. Low vitamin D can lead to rickets, bone deformities, swollen joints, and lameness; high vitamin D can cause vomiting, diarrhea, appetite loss, weakness, lethargy, reduced growth, weight loss, and potentially kidney calcification. Two rickets complaints have been reported. Pet owners should stop using the affected products immediately and contact Revival Animal Health at 1-877-870-4059 to arrange a full refund.
bodyHtml:
What happened

Revival Animal Health expanded its recall of canine milk replacers on June 25 after additional testing confirmed variable vitamin D levels across several product lines. The original recall was issued on April 17. The expansion adds Breeder's Edge Foster Care GM (goat milk) Milk Replacer to the list after testing found elevated vitamin D in that formula. Some recalled products contain too much vitamin D, while others contain too little. Two rickets complaints have been reported in connection with the affected products.

Affected products

  • Breeder's Edge Foster Care Canine Milk Replacer
  • Shelter's Choice Canine Milk Replacer
  • Breeder's Edge Foster Care GM (goat milk) Milk Replacer

The products were sold in various sizes nationwide through online channels and retail pet stores. Specific lot numbers or UPCs were not provided in the available reports.

Which pets are at risk

Puppies and dogs that consumed the affected milk replacers are at risk. Puppies with low vitamin D intake may develop rickets, bone deformities, swollen joints, and lameness. Dogs and puppies exposed to elevated vitamin D may experience vomiting, diarrhea, appetite loss, weakness, lethargy, reduced growth, weight loss, and, at very high levels, kidney calcification.

Why it matters

Vitamin D is essential for bone development and calcium regulation in puppies, but the concentration in these milk replacers is inconsistent. Either deficiency or excess can cause serious health problems, making this a potentially high-risk recall for young, developing puppies and vulnerable dogs.

What pet owners should do

Stop using the affected milk replacers immediately. Do not feed them to puppies or dogs. Contact Revival Animal Health at 1-877-870-4059 to request a full refund and follow any additional instructions from the company. If your pet has consumed any of the recalled products and shows signs of illness, contact your veterinarian promptly.

title: Pet Food Processing Q&A: Recall preparedness can prevent recalls
summary: Pet Food Processing published a Q&A on July 2, 2026, exploring how proactive recall preparedness can help pet food companies prevent recalls before they occur. The piece is an industry-focused discussion rather than a notice for a specific product recall; no brand, company, product name, lot code, UPC, or geographic distribution were provided in the reports. The summary tag identifies affected animals only as "other," with no species, breed, age, or condition specified. The article urges pet owners to be aware of the incident and take precautions, but it does not describe a specific contamination, injury, or illness event. Because concrete details are absent, owners should treat this as a general reminder to monitor pet food safety news, check recall announcements from manufacturers and regulators, and contact a veterinarian if a pet shows signs of illness after eating a recalled or suspect product. Until a specific product is named, no targeted removal from homes or refunds can be recommended.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On July 2, 2026, Pet Food Processing ran a Q&A article titled “Q&A: How recall preparedness can lead to recall prevention.” The piece is an industry discussion about how better preparation for potential recalls can reduce the chance that a recall happens in the first place. The available report excerpts do not describe a specific pet food recall, contamination finding, or adverse event. They only state that pet owners should be aware of the incident and take precautions, and list affected animals as “other.”

Affected products

No brand, product name, lot code, UPC, size, or best-by date was provided in the reports. The Q&A is not a product recall notice and does not name any specific food, treat, or supplement. Without additional details, it is not possible to identify which items, if any, should be removed from homes.

Which pets are at risk

The reports tag affected animals as “other,” but do not specify a species, breed, age, weight, or health condition. There is no mention of reported illnesses, injuries, or symptoms. The risk level cannot be assessed from the available information.

Why it matters

Recall preparedness is a core part of pet food safety. Strong traceability, supplier verification, testing protocols, and mock-recall programs can help manufacturers catch problems before products reach consumers. While the article itself does not announce a new recall, it reinforces that vigilance at the production level directly protects pets and owner confidence.

What pet owners should do

Because no specific product is named, pet owners should use this as a general safety reminder:

  • Monitor recall announcements from manufacturers, the FDA, and USDA.
  • Save pet food lot numbers and UPCs so you can quickly check alerts.
  • Watch pets for signs of illness such as vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, or loss of appetite, and contact a veterinarian if symptoms appear.
  • Report suspected pet food problems to the FDA’s Safety Reporting Portal or your local regulator.
  • No recall contact, refund phone number, or reimbursement process was provided in the reports. If a specific recall is announced later, follow the manufacturer’s instructions for returns and disposal.

title: Pedigree wet dog food recalled over sharp metal and plastic contamination
summary: Mars Petcare US, Inc. is voluntarily recalling two lots of 13.2-ounce Pedigree Can High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor wet dog food. The cans may contain sharp metal and plastic fragments, posing a choking, laceration, or gastrointestinal blockage risk to dogs. The lots originally failed quality checks and were sent to a third-party vendor for destruction, but were fraudulently diverted and sold through unauthorized U.S. channels, so the full distribution path is unknown. The recall affects dogs nationwide; Hawaii and other state health departments have issued warnings but cannot confirm local sales. No pet illnesses or injuries have been reported as of July 2. Dog owners should check the bottom of cans for lot codes 613C3KKCFC and 613C1KKCFC, stop feeding the recalled food immediately, and consult a veterinarian if their dog ate it. Replacements can be requested by calling Pedigree Consumer Care at 1-800-525-5273 (reported hours vary; some sources say 3 a.m.–3 p.m. Monday–Friday) or by visiting pedigree.com/update.
bodyHtml:
What happened

On July 2, 2026, Mars Petcare US, Inc. issued a voluntary recall of two lots of Pedigree Can High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor wet dog food. The products had failed the company’s quality and safety checks and were sent to a third-party vendor for destruction. The vendor allegedly diverted the cans and sold them through unauthorized U.S. retail channels, meaning recalled product may have reached stores nationwide. The issue was publicized by the FDA and state health agencies including the Hawaii Department of Health.

Affected products

  • Brand: PEDIGREE
  • Product: Pedigree Can High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor wet dog food
  • Size: 13.2-ounce cans
  • Lot codes (printed on the bottom of the can): 613C3KKCFC and 613C1KKCFC
  • No UPC or best-by dates were reported in the available notices.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs are the only animals affected. The recalled food may contain hard, sharp metal and plastic pieces that can injure the mouth, throat, or digestive tract.

Why it matters

Foreign material contamination can cause choking, cuts/lacerations, or gastrointestinal blockages requiring veterinary care. No dog illnesses or injuries had been reported as of the July 2 recall announcement.

What pet owners should do

  • Stop feeding the recalled product immediately.
  • Check the bottom of Pedigree cans for lot codes 613C3KKCFC or 613C1KKCFC.
  • Do not feed the food if it matches the recalled lots.
  • Contact a veterinarian if your dog ate the food or shows signs of distress, vomiting, choking, or lethargy.
  • For a replacement or assistance, contact Pedigree Consumer Care at 1-800-525-5273 (some sources list hours as 3 a.m.–3 p.m. Monday–Friday) or visit pedigree.com/update.

title: Poisoned cat food left at Dryden mobile home park; dog sickened
summary: WSTM reports that approximately 40 cans of poisoned cat food were left at a mobile home park in Dryden, and at least one dog has been sickened after exposure. The brand, manufacturer, and exact product name have not been identified, and no lot numbers or UPC codes are available. Both dogs and cats are considered affected animals because the contaminated food was placed in an outdoor area accessible to local pets. The incident appears to be an intentional poisoning event rather than a manufacturer recall, and authorities are warning pet owners in the area to remain alert. The geographic scope is centered on the mobile home park in Dryden, with broader caution urged for residents who walk or let pets outside nearby. Pet owners should check their yards and common areas for unfamiliar food or containers, keep cats indoors and dogs on leashes, and prevent pets from scavenging. If a pet shows possible poisoning signs—such as vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, tremors, seizures, or difficulty breathing—seek emergency veterinary care immediately. Anyone who finds suspicious food or has information about how it was placed should contact local law enforcement or animal control. No recall hotline, refund process, or manufacturer contact has been provided.
bodyHtml:
What happened

WSTM reported that 40 cans of poisoned cat food were left at a mobile home park in Dryden, and that a dog was sickened. Authorities warned pet owners in the area to be aware of the incident and to take precautions. The report did not identify a suspect, motive, or the exact manner in which the dog was exposed.

Affected products

No brand, manufacturer, specific product name, lot code, or UPC is available. The items are described only as 40 cans of cat food that were poisoned or tainted.

Which pets are at risk

Dogs and cats are identified as the affected animals. Outdoor pets and those allowed to roam or scavenge near the mobile home park face the highest risk of exposure.

Why it matters

Deliberate poisoning poses an acute, potentially fatal danger to pets if they consume the contaminated food. Because the food was placed in a residential area, multiple animals could be exposed. The lack of brand or product details prevents a targeted recall or consumer warning from a manufacturer.

What pet owners should do

Inspect outdoor areas, keep cats indoors, and keep dogs leashed or supervised. Do not let pets eat any found food, scraps, or unfamiliar containers. Watch for signs of poisoning such as vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, tremors, seizures, or difficulty breathing, and seek emergency veterinary care immediately if any appear. Report suspicious food or containers to local law enforcement or animal control. No recall contact, refund process, or manufacturer hotline has been provided.
